title: The reasons for international trade
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS international trade — comparative advantage, gains from trade, terms of trade, and GeoGebra trade model.
learningObjectives: Show gains from trade on PPF diagrams.
Define and interpret terms of trade.
Evaluate limitations including transport costs and protectionism.

simpleExplanation.title: Why countries trade
simpleExplanation.summary: Countries gain by specialising where opportunity cost is lowest and trading for other goods. Consumption can exceed domestic production possibilities.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Even if you are faster at both cooking and cleaning than your housemate, you still split tasks — each focuses on what they give up least to do, and both benefit from the arrangement.
steps: Comparative advantage: lower opportunity cost, not absolute cost.
Trade allows consumption beyond pre-trade PPF.
Terms of trade = index of export prices / import prices.
Barriers: tariffs, quotas — reduce gains from specialisation.

flashcards.back: Absolute: produce more with same resources. Comparative: lower opportunity cost — basis for trade even if one country is absolutely better at everything.
What must be given up of Good A to produce one more unit of Good B — country with lower OC has comparative advantage in B.
Specialisation and exchange allow consumption combinations outside the pre-trade PPF — both countries can consume more than autarky.
Index of export prices ÷ index of import prices × 100. Improvement means export prices rise relative to import prices.

flashcards.front: When does trade benefit both countries?
flashcards.back: When the terms of trade lie between the two countries' opportunity cost ratios — each gains by exporting its comparative advantage good.
Transport costs, immobile factors, protectionism, and unequal gains (some groups lose from specialisation).

sections.content: **International trade** (6.1) opens the **international economics** unit. Master **opportunity cost ratios** — Cambridge frequently sets numerical comparative advantage questions.

Country A has a **comparative advantage** in a good if it can produce it at a **lower opportunity cost** than Country B — even if A is less efficient in absolute terms.

Each country should **specialise** in the good with comparative advantage and **trade** for the other.

Opportunity cost of 1 unit of X = $\frac{\text{Units of Y given up}}{\text{Units of X gained}}$

Terms of trade index = $\frac{\text{Export price index}}{\text{Import price index}} \times 100$

sections.question:
Country P and Country Q produce wheat and cloth. Output per worker per year:

| | Wheat | Cloth |
|---|---|---|
| P | 30 | 20 |
| Q | 20 | 40 |

(a) Which country has absolute advantage in each good?
(b) Calculate opportunity cost ratios.
(c) Identify comparative advantage and recommend specialisation.
sections.solution:
(a) **Absolute advantage**
**P** in wheat (30 > 20). **Q** in cloth (40 > 20).

(b) **Opportunity costs**
In **P**: 1 wheat costs 20/30 = **0.67 cloth**; 1 cloth costs 30/20 = **1.5 wheat**
In **Q**: 1 wheat costs 40/20 = **2 cloth**; 1 cloth costs 20/40 = **0.5 wheat**

(c) **Comparative advantage and specialisation**
**P** has lower OC in wheat (0.67 < 2) → **comparative advantage in wheat**
**Q** has lower OC in cloth (0.5 < 1.5) → **comparative advantage in cloth**

**P specialises in wheat; Q specialises in cloth** — trade at terms of trade between 0.67 and 2 cloth per wheat benefits both.

items: **Improvement** in terms of trade: export prices rise relative to imports — country can buy more imports per export unit.
**Deterioration**: import prices rise faster — worsens trade balance if volumes unchanged.
**Primary producers** often face deteriorating terms of trade (Prebisch-Singer thesis — evaluation point).

sections.content: Always show **opportunity cost calculations** in comparative advantage questions — marks are awarded for working, not just stating who specialises in what.

steps.caption: Specialisation: countries produce at lower opportunity cost.
steps.embedHint: Compare OC ratios — export good with comparative advantage.

steps.caption: Gains from trade: consumption beyond domestic PPF.
steps.embedHint: Post-trade consumption point outside pre-trade frontier.

steps.caption: Terms of trade: export price / import price index.
steps.embedHint: Improvement if export prices rise relative to imports.

steps.caption: Limitations: transport costs, protectionism, immobile factors.
steps.embedHint: Show trade blocked when cost exceeds gain from specialisation.
